data.title: Madison Investors Fund Q3 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
The Madison Investors Fund (Class Y) increased 7.80% in the third quarter of 2024, compared to the 5.89% increase in the S&P 500 index. In the year-to-date nine-month period, the Fund returned 16.96%, compared to the S&P 500’s 22.08% return.

Portfolio Performance

In the third quarter, the top five individual contributors to performance relative to the benchmark were Parker-Hannifin Corporation (PH), Fiserv (FI), Lowe’s Companies (LOW), Brookfield Corporation (BN), and Progressive Corporation (PGR).

Parker-Hannifin is a leading diversified industrial manufacturer. Despite the unfavorable backdrop of a slowing industrial economy, the company continues to execute well with respect to improving margins and integrating the recent acquisition of Meggitt. At payment processor Fiserv, revenue and profits continue to grow nicely, in part driven by the strong performance of its Clover payment platform for small-and-medium sized businesses. In our assessment, Clover’s broad adoption

by merchants across a wide variety of verticals is due to its superior functionality compared to legacy point-of-sale payment platforms, coupled with Fiserv’s extensive distribution scale across financial institutions and independent sales organizations.

This latter advantage is especially difficult for emerging fintech competitors to replicate, reinforcing our confidence in the company’s long-term prospects.

Despite operating in very different sectors, Lowe’s Companies and Brookfield Corporation are both expected to benefit from the economic activity spurred on by declining interest rates. The Federal Reserve’s decision to lower interest rates sparked investor enthusiasm for both companies during the quarter, even as their sales and profits continue to moderate. For Lowe’s, sales remained weak in the latest quarter as most measures of the housing market remain sluggish. However, if interest rates come down and mortgages become more affordable, activity should return to the housing market, which will boost Lowe’s business. Similarly, Brookfield’s asset management subsidiary saw fee revenue growth slow due to reduced levels of activity in private markets. We believe stable or declining interest rates will revive private market activity, benefiting Brookfield Corporation’s business.

Rounding out the top five contributors is Progressive, an insurance company that continues to benefit from attractive market conditions and share gains from less advantaged competitors.

The bottom five individual detractors were Dollar Tree (DLTR), Alphabet (GOOG), PACCAR (PCAR), Charles Schwab (SCHW), and Copart (CPRT).

Dollar Tree underperformed following disappointing sales at the core Dollar Tree banner and reduced full-year earnings guidance. The company, as well as its closest peers, is facing headwinds from a weak low-end consumer, less ‘trade-down’ benefit from middle-income consumers, and a tough competitive environment. Despite these headwinds, we are encouraged by the long-term prospects of the multi-price initiatives at the Dollar Tree banner, with the latest iteration of updated stores showing a strong up-lift in sales. As management more aggressively rolls out these updates, the impact to the company will be more meaningful, and, we believe, result in much higher earnings power.

Over the last few years, investors have oscillated between valuing Alphabet as an “AI Winner” and “AI Loser”. This quarter it was something different, a court ruling in the antitrust suit brought by the Department of Justice, that scared investors. While we believe Alphabet can absorb a wide range of regulatory outcomes, we will remain vigilant in our assessment of the company. Despite all the headlines around the legal case and competitive inroads from AI chatbots, the businesses’ underlying performance continues to be strong across Search, YouTube, and Cloud.

After initially holding up well against declining freight rates and a softening economic backdrop, sales at truck manufacturer PACCAR have begun to weaken. We think the company’s high-margin, stable aftermarket parts division will offer some support through the current cyclical downturn.

At Charles Schwab, a discount securities broker, investors remain focused on the higher cost of funding the business. While the short-term impact of customers ‘sorting’ cash into higher-yielding money market funds and fixed income securities presents a headwind to earnings, we believe Schwab will continue to gobble up market share. Over time, we expect profits will normalize higher as these challenges stabilize and the business grows.

Copart’s steady earnings growth took a pause this quarter as margins were hit by elevated growth in expenses. While some of the costs will prove to be more one-off in nature, management is investing behind growth and efficiency initiatives, which we believe will prove to be capital well spent.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we purchased one new position and sold one.

We purchased a stake in Keysight Technologies (KEYS). Keysight is a leading electronic test and measurement company with a reputation for manufacturing highly reliable oscilloscopes, network analyzers, signal generators, and spectrum analyzers. Keysight serves a wide range of customers across telecommunication, data centers, semiconductors, and industrial markets. Its instruments are absolutely mission-critical for the research and development as well as deployment of just about anything with sophisticated electronic componentry or signals. For instance, without Keysight’s instruments, telecommunication carriers couldn’t deploy 5G networks and semiconductor manufacturers couldn’t test the performance of their circuitry. Customers are very sticky and brand loyal, as it takes years to establish a good reputation among engineers. This means that Keysight primarily competes on the quality of its products rather than price.

Over the long term, the global economy will continue to digitize, which means that the accompanying need to precisely measure electronic signals will only increase. As a result, we believe that Keysight’s end markets will grow in excess of GDP. However, Keysight is currently experiencing an air pocket in demand. The initial rush of spending to deploy 5G networks has coincided with softening demand across automotive, general electronics, and semiconductor end markets. While we don’t precisely know how long the soft environment will last, we are confident that Keysight’s sales and profits will return to an attractive growth rate. The poor sentiment from the currently depressed demand environment presented the opportunity to purchase shares at a discount to our appraisal of intrinsic value.

During the quarter, we sold our stake in Brookfield Asset Management, a leading alternative asset manager. In late 2022, we received shares in the company following its spin-off from Brookfield Corporation. We admire Brookfield Asset Management’s owner-operator culture and long-term track record of generating impressive investment performance. However, during the quarter, we sold our stake to fund other opportunities. We continue to be optimistic on the outlook for Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) and have indirect exposure to the company though our stake in Brookfield Corporation, which remains Brookfield Asset Management’s largest shareholder.

We thank you for your trust and remain invested alongside you for the long term.

Respectfully,

Rich Eisinger | Haruki Toyama | Joe Maginot

Disclosures

Indices are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. They are shown for illustrative purposes only, and do not represent the performance of any specific investment. Index returns do not include any expenses, fees or sales charges, which would lower performance.

The S&P 500® Index is a large-cap market index which measures the performance of a representative sample of 500 leading companies in leading industries in the U.S.

“Madison” and/or “Madison Investments” is the unifying tradename of Madison Investment Holdings, Inc., Madison Asset Management, LLC (“MAM”), and Madison Investment Advisors, LLC (“MIA”). MAM and MIA are registered as investment advisers with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. Madison Funds are distributed by MFD Distributor, LLC. MFD Distributor, LLC is registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as a broker-dealer and is a member firm of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority. The home office for each firm listed above is 550 Science Drive, Madison, WI 53711. Madison’s toll-free number is 800-767-0300.

Any performance data shown represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Non-deposit investment products are not federally insured, involve investment risk, may lose value and are not obligations of, or guaranteed by, any financial institution. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate.

This report is for informational purposes only and is not intended as an offer or solicitation with respect to the purchase or sale of any security.

Consider the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of Madison Funds carefully before investing. Each fund’s prospectus contains this and other information about the fund. Call 800.877.6089 or visit Madison Funds to obtain a prospectus and read it carefully before investing.

Although the information in this report has been obtained from sources that the firm believes to be reliable, we do not guarantee its accuracy, and any such information may be incomplete or condensed. All opinions included in the report constitute the authors’ judgment as of the date of this report and are subject to change without notice. This report is for informational purposes only and is not intended as an offer or solicitation with respect to the purchase or sale of any security.

Madison Asset Management, LLC does not provide investment advice directly to shareholders of the Madison Funds. Opinions stated are informational only and should not be taken as investment recommendation or advice of any kind whatsoever (whether impartial or otherwise). Madison Funds are distributed by MFD Distributor, LLC, member FINRA.

Madison-618050-2024-10-08

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CSQ And CII: Attractive Distributions With Solid Track Records
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Closed-end funds often pay relatively higher distribution yields. This is often because they rely on not only distributing income but also capital gains and return of capital distributions. The return of capital distributions can be destructive or non-destructive, which is going to primarily be determined by the direction of the net asset value per share over time.

Today, I wanted to provide two potential CEF opportunities that have been able to deliver attractive distributions — although relatively more modest compared to some peers — that have also been able to maintain their NAV over the long haul. These funds will still require capital gains to fund their distributions to shareholders, but they've been experiencing a trend of rising NAV since the Global Financial Crisis. Enough of an increase during this time that each now has a higher NAV per share than they had at inception.

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (NASDAQ:CSQ)

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.67
  • Discount/Premium: -2.83%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.17%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.57%
  • Leverage: 29.47%
  • Managed Assets: $4.013 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

CSQ's objective is to seek "total return through a combination of capital appreciation and current income." They attempt to achieve this simply by "investing in a diversified portfolio of equities, convertible securities and high yield corporate bonds."

The fund has the ability to invest where they think the best opportunities might be. That's part of the appeal of active management: The flexibility to adapt to different situations. However, it should be noted that the fund will have "at least 50% in equity securities."

The last time I touched on Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund was when I was adding to my position in August's market sell-off. At that time, the Nasdaq Composite briefly touched correction territory with a 10%-plus decline. Though the index quickly recovered, it hasn't reached new heights yet. The S&P 500 Index experienced a more shallow decline but has gone on to hit new all-time highs more recently.

CSQ isn't a pure equity fund that will always follow the general market exactly as it has more of a hybrid approach, investing in convertibles and other fixed-income instruments. However, it will follow the general trend of the market, with the added potential benefit of leverage at play. Of course, with that leverage also comes greater volatility and higher downside risk if things aren't going so hot in the broader market.

Since our last focused update on this fund back in May, CSQ has delivered about the same total returns as the S&P 500 Index.

During that time, the fund maintained its fairly shallow discount. Even with the greater volatility we experienced in the last couple of months, that stubborn tight discount has remained.

On the other hand, the fund had been flirting with a premium since around 2018, trending higher until about 2022 before it started to trend sideways to slightly lower.

When I added to my position, it was due to the broader market pullback and not really that the discount had opened up meaningfully during the time. This fund has delivered an impressive track record. Like many funds, it took a big hit during the Global Financial Crisis. That said, it has clawed back the decline and is one of the more select few CEFs with a higher share and NAV price now than at its inception.

It has been trending higher since the GFC and also delivering an attractive distribution to investors. They had maintained their monthly pay schedule since inception, though that does include significant reductions in the payout the fund saw in 2008/2009. This was pretty common amongst CEFs that had an inception prior to the GFC and are still around today. Only a small handful of funds can boast of never cutting their payout during that time.

Currently, the distribution rate works out to 7.17%. Given the shallow discount, the NAV rate is also quite similar at 6.97%. Since the fund's inception to Oct. 15, 2024, the fund has paid out a total of $20.532 in distributions to its investors. The fund's inception NAV was $14.31, so the fund has distributed out well more than its inception value and also trades above that level today.

BlackRock Enhanced Capital and Income Fund (NYSE:CII)

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.00
  • Discount/Premium: -8.64%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.14%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.89%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $919 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

CII has a simple objective and strategy. It “seeks to provide investors with a combination of current income and capital appreciation” by “investing in a portfolio of equity securities of the U.S. and foreign issuers.” They will also “employ a strategy of writing call and put options.”

BlackRock Enhanced Capital and Income Fund is another closed-end fund with a rather solid historical track record. Similar to CSQ, it has delivered attractive distributions and its NAV per share trades above its inception NAV per share while the inception goes back to early 2004. The inception NAV for this fund comes to $19.10 with a share price at IPO of $20.

That said, the share price is still slightly off from where it started out. It also followed a similar trajectory of going much lower during the GFC before trending higher since those years.

Being a non-leveraged fund, one could consider CII to be relatively more conservative. The fund invests entirely in equity positions and writes covered calls on a portion of the underlying individual holdings. That's one of the ways it helps to generate the attractive monthly distribution that CII has implemented since near the end of 2014. Prior to that, the fund had paid on a quarterly distribution schedule.

The distribution rate currently works out to 6.14% for CII, but unlike CSQ, CII is trading at a meaningful discount. That means the fund's NAV distribution rate comes in at a more modest 5.61%. As I had highlighted previously, I believe that CII could be due for a distribution boost.

However, aside from the potential for an increased distribution at some point, we have another opportunity to participate in a tender offer for CII. This was triggered as the fund had an average discount wider than -7.50% during the second measurement period of the fund's discount management program. For CII, the average discount was -8.31% from July 1, 2024, until the end of September 2024.

We previously discussed the discount management program more in-depth, but the more important point is that it's a tender offer for 2.5% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV. If the fund continues to trade at a discount greater than 2%, then it would make sense to participate—not factoring for potential tax obligations, which will be different for each investor.

For CII, that doesn't appear to be an issue with the fund's sizeable discount and participating now makes sense. However, we have until Nov. 15, 2024, the tender offer expiration. Brokers generally have cut-off times that are a day earlier than this, so always check with your own broker to determine when the decision has to be made.

While the 2.5% sounds small, not all investors participate in tender offers, and that can be due to a variety of different reasons. They don't want to have to deal with the tax consequences, or the tax consequences might make it an unprofitable move. Discounts could narrow to a level where investors may simply choose to sell their entire position—which can happen more in larger tender offers that can often see meaningful discount reductions.

Further, some investors just simply don't pay attention to what they're investing in and what's going on with the funds.

Whatever the reason, that benefits those of us who do participate. In the first round, CII ended up accepting 18.88% of shares from investors who participated with 100% of their position. That works out to about 7.5x what would be the minimum accepted amount of 2.5%. It's never guaranteed that this next tender offer will experience the same level, but it is almost certain to be higher than the 2.5% stated.

Conclusion

CSQ and CII have been able to deliver solid distributions over the years, even if they pay more modest distributions. In the end, the distribution level isn't necessarily what matters, as whatever isn't going to be paid is simply going to be going to the underlying NAV. Thus, it's why we've seen these funds with more realistic distributions— and not being afraid to have to adjust when necessary, such as during the GFC — at higher NAVs now than at inception.

Of course, there were quite a number of years after the GFC that it took these funds to get back to that level. However, the GFC has remained, so far, a once-in-a-lifetime event. COVID saw a sharp drop but the recovery was swift enough that there wasn't permanent damage done to these funds. It isn't to say that these funds won't ever go back below NAV, as they're both at the mercy of primarily equity markets overall. In the case of CSQ, that includes being leveraged, which leads to the fund having amplified moves in both directions.

With all that said, I believe that both CSQ and CII continue to be strong choices for the long-term investor. They could be more appropriate for those looking for an attractive monthly payout but balancing that distribution with the potential appreciation.

data.title: Logista: Upside In Pharmaceuticals And M&A Despite Tobacco Risks
data.description:
Air freight and logistics are an essential part of trade and is what keeps the world running the way we know it now. However, it is definitely not an easy sector to be active in. Over the past year, we have seen many companies facing an overcapacity on the market leading to an accelerated normalization of unit prices on the markets while the companies had significantly expanded during the pandemic to service demand. We are currently seeing some stabilization, but we also do note that China’s QDP hit the lowest growth rate since 2023. If you want to be invested, not all air freight and logistics providers may make sense and it is important to consider the prospects of other businesses within the sector as well to provide a geographical diversification. In this report, I will be adding Logista Integral to my coverage and assess whether the company’s stock is a buy, sell or hold.

Logista: A Tobacco And Phamaceuticals Transport Specialist in Western Europe

Logista Integral is a company headquartered in Spain providing transport services for books, tobacco, pharameceuticals and convenient products to 200,000 points of sale in Spain, Portugal, Italy, France, the Benelux and Poland. Via its fully owned subsidiaries Nacex, Logista Parcel and Logista freight the company also provides parcel, express delivery and temperature controlled transport services in Spain and Portugal as well as full load transport for high value products in a temperature controlled environment in the EU. In 2023, the company expanded its business with the acquisition of Gramma Farmaceutici which expands its overall distribution channel and more importantly in Italy. The company also distributes electronically rechargeable products and services such as gift cards, parking cards and prepaid cards in France and Spain while also providing printing services. So, Logista is not a pure play on freight and logistics and parts of its business are focused on extracting value down the chain.

In 2023, the company reported sales of €12.4 billion with an adjusted operating profit of €366 million indicating a 2.9% adjusted operating margin.

Logista Earnings Show A Low Margin Business

Revenues for the nine months ended grew 3.6% to €9.48 billion and was primarily driven by growth in Spain, Portugal, Poland and the Benelux with sales growth of 7.4% with strong growth in tobacco and related product transport and pharmaceutical distribution. In Italy, revenues increased by 2.6% to €3.2 billion while sales in France were flat at €2.8 billion. Gross profit increased by 4.6%, driven by positive inventory changes while operating costs increased by 4.2% largely reflecting the operating costs of the logistics network. Adjusted EBIT increased 6.2% to €290.2 million while operating profit increased 14.8% to €248.9 million indicating margins of 2.6% compared to 2.4% to the comparable period last year.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Logista?

The opportunities for Logista are growth of its throughput in its existing network and expansion by means of M&A to increase its distribution channels. However, I believe that the risks are somewhat bigger for Logista. A significant part of its business focuses on transport services for tobacco. Stricter regulation on tobacco sales could negatively affect a significant part of Logista’s business while the company, like many other logistics companies, is prone to macroeconomic factors and cost increases on labor and fuel.

Especially with declining tobacco consumption, Logista likely has to pivot other means of revenues such as pharmaceuticals and express delivery which also sees fierce competition. Looking at the margins for Logista, there is not much to be impressed with. Ideally, we would like to say that low margins are due to the high cost inflation over the past years. However, pre-pandemic the margins were about 2% and since the lockdowns eased the margins have climbed to roughly 3%. Whether it is 2% or 3%, the conclusion is that Logista is a very low-margin business and I am not quite interested in investing in low-margin businesses for the simple reason that as a logistics provider the company is somewhat more exposed to macroeconomic fluctuations and its margins don’t really provide a nice padding.

Logista Has Upside On Peer Group Valuation

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Logista has some elements that I am not a big fan of. The first one is that the company has a very low margin and the second one is that exposure to tobacco which could see some pressures if governments increase regulation on tobacco sales. However, there definitely is one thing that I do like and that is the fact that the company has virtually no debt and with a positive free cash flow it can return value to shareholders through stock repurchases and dividends. Furthermore, the company can utilize its free cash flow to acquire companies and for instance increase its pharmaceuticals business. Based on the company’s median EV/EBITDA, the stock is overpriced at current levels. However, I do think that a valuation in line with peers can be justified given the absence of debt. As a result, I rate the stock a buy with a $36.12 price target indicating 21% upside. For those that are interested in acquiring shares I would recommend checking out the listing in at the Madrid Stock Exchange where it has better liquidity than its CDNIF OTC listing.

Conclusion: Logista Has Some Appeal

Logista is definitely not my favorite investment in the logistics space. However, what I definitely do like is the fact that the company has no debt and positive free cash flow. That provides the company with a lot of opportunities to generate value for shareholders either through share repurchases or dividend payments and increases or through expansion of its business activities. With that in mind, I do believe a case for a peer group valuation can be made.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Boeing: The Brand Strength Is Unrivalled
data.description:
Would you feel comfortable flying at a 6-mile altitude in a Chinese-made COMAC?

This is probably the most important question that we have to answer when judging the prospects of Boeing (NYSE:BA).

If your answer is yes, you probably don’t have to continue reading this article… however, we do believe that Boeing has something enduring, something that is sure to help the business bounce back, and something that is completely ignored by professional analysts today.

It is very tempting to seek a fake sense of certainty in elaborate financial models, but would you actually feel more comfortable flying on some no-name plane if they gave you a spreadsheet on how good the plane was? There is something in common between investing and air travel I guess.

Financial markets are very competitive. Hundreds of Ivy League-educated analysts, industry experts and investment bankers are scrutinising every number reported by U.S. bluechip stocks such as Boeing, hoping to find a good deal. How could we expect to outmanoeuvre this fine roster of spreadsheet-bearing experts?

Here is a hint for you: if you want to win don’t play chess with Bobby Fischer. We can analyse the numbers and project financials as much as we want, but our forecasts will not be better than the consensus. If we aim to outperform the passive index trackers, spreadsheets won’t help.

And that is why our opening question is so important. The Boeing Company is struggling financially at the moment. The numbers aren’t pretty, but isn’t it a great company actually?

I invite my readers to ponder about this along with me.

Could Boeing be among the strongest brands ever?

Thousands of people entrust their lives to Boeing daily. In the overwhelming majority of cases, Boeing has gotten us there safely, many times. We therefore trust Boeing, not even advertising is needed for this brand.

Perhaps strangely, Boeing reminds us of Coca-Cola. Our attachment to this brand is also rooted in deep personal experiences. I still remember the first time I tasted Coca-Cola, it was just so much better than all the other drinks I had tried before. I was hooked for life.

All of us know what Boeing is, all of us have flown their planes dozens of times, and all of these trips have gone mostly smoothly, otherwise, you would not be reading this.

Boeing stands for safety and reliability. This brand is entrenched by decades of personal experiences, with hundreds of millions of safe passages completed.

We were conditioned from childhood to feel safe in these sky-piercing ‘metal tubes’ manufactured by Boeing.

We remain attached to the products that give us great initial experiences, even if we grow weary of them eventually. I can barely taste Coke anymore, but I still drink it and I do not really know why. However, when I try some private-label cola, I do remember why I liked Coke in the first place.

Most of us are also so accustomed to the safety provided by Boeing, to the point that we do not appreciate it any more. However, our lives are worth more than a 50-cent Kirkland cola can, and most of us are unwilling to take to the skies on some no-name Chinese plane.

The brand's strength keeps customers coming back even when some of its products disappoint. If Coca-Cola has managed to survive the “New Coke’ debacle, I am pretty sure that Boeing will also weather the recent issues.

There have been crashes and, unfortunately, there will be more. But the overwhelming majority of us had a safe experience. Boeing will maintain a dominant position in the commercial aviation industry despite all the recent setbacks. We will continue trusting Boeing planes.

No matter how much COMAC spend on marketing, billions or trillions, they will not be able to re-create the goodwill that Boeing has. The strength of the Boeing brand is unrivalled.

The current issues present a unique opportunity to invest in this high-quality business. Boeing is currently trading near the lows, but it is as strong as before.

Boeing is going through a rough patch, but sales will recover

The current issues of Being were well covered by the media and there are plenty, to be honest. Production delays and quality issues have affected the 737 Max, 787 Dreamliner, and 777X aircraft programmes. At the moment labour disputes are creating added delays, while the defence division is struggling with cost overruns.

The labour strike is costing Boeing more than $1 billion per month while the defence division is also draining cash. The company is now planning to raise $10 billion worth of equity to improve its financial position.

Baron Rothschild famously said: “buy property when there’s blood in the streets”, I guess he would also be pondering the idea of investing in Boeing now.

We have over 15 years of experience in financial markets and spent most of this time pursuing fundamentally strong businesses that hit a rough patch.

We have learned that in situations with elevated uncertainty detailed financial modelling not only offers little value but can often be misleading and create a fake sense of certainty. General rules of thumb serve investors and forecasters best when dealing with elevated uncertainty.

All value stocks trade at attractive valuation multiples, but price alone is not a good reason to invest. Risk management is the most essential part of value investing and there are two factors of utmost importance when judging the risk profile of a value investment idea:

  1. The business needs to have a strong competitive position and their products/services should not become obsolete.
  2. The industry in which the business operates needs to be growing.

Despite the current issues, Boeing fares well on both of these counts.

  1. Boeing commercial aircraft will continue being demanded worldwide due to their decades-long track record for safety and reliability.
  2. Air traffic passenger volumes continue to expand globally, driven by middle-class and population growth in China, India and Southeast Asia. Global passenger volume is expected to grow by 3.8% annually over the next 20 years according to International Air Transport Association (IATA).

Despite the operational issues, Boeing still has ~7 years’ worth of order backlog. The company’s product is still highly desired, it is not becoming obsolete and demand is growing.

The airlines demand new planes to stay competitive and also due to growing passenger volumes. There are only two internationally certified suppliers Boeing and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) that can satisfy this demand. Neither one of them is able to fill all orders by itself.

Boeing planes will continue being demanded, and due to the market growth, the company’s sales volumes are likely to stay resilient even if it loses some market share.

When operational issues are fully worked out, the revenues and the profits will recover and continue growing with the industry.

Boeing is trading at an all-time discount to order backlog

The strike will end eventually and quality issues will be resolved. It is likely that, the cost of manufacturing Boeing planes will increase tough.

Neither of the two dominant suppliers has exorbitant pricing power. After all, Boeing would not want to alienate their loyal customers. Having said it, Boeing can pass on some cost increases to its customers.

Many aircraft purchase agreements include escalation clauses, which allow for price adjustments based on changes in key cost factors, such as inflation, labour costs, or material prices. Escalation clauses provide some protection for Boeing against cost increases, but they are often capped at certain percentages and may not cover extreme or unexpected cost surges.

Before the pandemic and 737 Max crashes, Boeing was delivering close to 800 planes per annum and generating close to $10 billion of operating earnings on $100 billion in revenues.

Boeing annual reports, TIKR Terminal

The operating profit margin of Boeing likely will come under pressure within the next few years, as the company will have to absorb some cost increases itself. Boeing is likely to generate profit margins below those seen before the COVID-19 pandemic. The management is guiding for a high single-digit margin and we believe this is a reasonable assumption.

When aeroplane deliveries return to the 800+ per annum mark, Boeing is very likely to generate $7- $8 billion of operating profits. At a 20X earnings multiple, Boeing would then be worth upwards of $230 per share. It currently trades at ~$150, offering a 50% upside.

Nobody knows exactly how long it will take for Boeing to come back to a smooth operation or when the bottom in the stock price will be wrecked. Stock market timing is close to impossible and we will not claim otherwise. However it is quite clear that this great business, and it is undervalued.

The risks

There are risks, but they mainly relate to timing. Increased FAA scrutiny might hold back production for longer than expected delaying the payback to our investment. Defence and Space division could face further struggles if the Department of Defense is not willing to renegotiate the fixed-price development contracts. Neither of these issues will sink the company, however.

Boeing will soar again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: No Safety Margin (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Kinder Morgan, Inc. (NYSE:KMI) submitted a decent earnings scorecard for the third fiscal quarter that showed continual momentum in EBITDA and its natural gas business especially. The natural gas-focused energy company lowered its guidance for FY 2024, however, due to lower crude volumes and commodity prices, and also delays in the ramp of natural gas facilities. Despite the lowered EBITDA outlook, I believe Kinder Morgan is a top midstream play for investors that want to focus on sustainable DCF and EBITDA growth. Since the valuation has reached my fair value target, however, I am down-grading shares to hold following the release of third-quarter earnings.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Kinder Morgan a strong buy previously as I saw an upside revaluation scenario related to the company's deleveraging: A Solid, 5.4% Yielding Midstream Growth Play. Another reason why I recommended Kinder Morgan last year was that the midstream firm had considerable acquisition potential. In Q3'24, Kinder Morgan delivered low single-digit growth in EBITDA, driven by growth in natural gas, but was forced to lower its guidance for FY 2024 due to headwinds in the crude oil business. The dividend coverage profile remained healthy in the third quarter, however.

Kinder Morgan's Q3'24 results

The pipeline company missed EPS estimates for the third fiscal quarter by $0.01 per share, but its key metric -- adjusted EBITDA -- showed healthy growth. Kinder Morgan's adjusted EBITDA grew 2% year-over-year to $1.88B, mainly due to strong performance in the core natural gas business.

Natural gas is the bread and butter of Kinder Morgan's multidimensional business model and the midstream firm has been determined in strengthening this business over time through acquisitions and CapEx allocations. According to the midstream firm's FY 2024 business plan, natural gas is expected to represent 57% of Kinder Morgan's segment earnings.

The natural gas business offers long-term opportunities for growth, especially as energy demands are only set to grow. These growing energy needs in large part come from the growth of the Data Center sector: Data Centers are getting bigger and are requiring more energy, mostly due to compute and energy-intensive AI workloads which leads to healthy demand projections for the natural gas sector.

Natural Gas already generates the majority of Kinder Morgan segment earnings: 64% of the midstream firm's earnings before deprecation and amortization came solely from its natural gas pipelines. Natural gas generated 7% earnings growth year-over-year, resulting in segment EBDA of $1.28B. Total segment earnings amounted to $1.97B, showing 2% growth year-over-year, so Kinder Morgan's natural gas business is greatly out-performing the rest of the company's segments which include products pipelines, terminals and CO2.

FY 2024 guidance reduction

Lower crude volumes, pressure on commodity prices (especially crude oil) and delays in the ramp-up of facilities in the natural gas business forced Kinder Morgan to revise its outlook for FY 2024 EBITDA, but only slightly and not enough to fundamentally change the trajectory of Kinder Morgan's business.

The midstream firm now expects to be below budgeted adjusted EBITDA (by 2%) in FY 2024 and miss its adjusted EPS by approximately 4%. Still, Kinder Morgan expects its adjusted EBITDA to grow 5% and its adjusted EPS to grow 9% compared to FY 2023.

Dividend coverage profile

Kinder Morgan presented a resilient dividend coverage profile for the third quarter, no surprises here. The natural gas business especially results in highly predictable results due to the contracted nature of its cash flows. In the third fiscal quarter, Kinder Morgan generated $0.49 per share in distributable cash flow which was calculated to a dividend coverage ratio of 1.7X.

KMI

(Source: Author)

Valuation

Kinder Morgan is now one of the more expensive midstream firms in the sector which includes my all-time favorite Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD). Kinder Morgan trades at an ~11.0X enterprise-value-to-EBITDA which is well above the company's longer term average.

The EV-to-EBITDA ratio ignores earnings-distorting depreciation and amortization expenses, but also recognizes that companies in the midstream sector owe a lot of debt, much of which is accumulated over time due to acquisitions. Therefore, the EV-to-EBITDA ratio is very useful in valuing midstream companies.

Enterprise Products Partners is now significantly more expensive than its historical valuation indicates as well which is a result of the company's surging share price in 2024: in the last three years, Kinder Morgan was valued at an average enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of only 9.5X, so shares are now trading at a 15% premium to the historical average.

In my last work on Kinder Morgan in September I stated that the midstream firm could have a fair value EV-to-EBITDA ratio of 10-11X which implied a fair value of $21.50 to $23.60 per share. This fair value has now been exceeded as shares are currently trading at $24.72.

Since I am a long-term investor and focus on generating recurring income from Kinder Morgan's shares, am I not recommending to sell, however. Instead, I am lowering my rating to hold, but would consider adding to my current position at a price of $20 or below. Since the top-end of my fair value range is surpassed, I believe an investment at this point does not provide investors with a safety margin.

Risks with Kinder Morgan

Kinder Morgan is a natural gas-focused midstream play with considerable operations in other segments as well. However, the company will remain dependent on natural gas as this business makes the biggest EBDA and EBITDA contributions and is growing the fastest. What would change my mind about Kinder Morgan is if the company were to see slowing volume growth in the natural gas business or if it saw a deterioration in its dividend coverage profile.

Closing thoughts

Kinder Morgan slightly lowered its guidance for FY 2024 in terms of adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS, but only marginally. Kinder Morgan's Q3 EBITDA kept growing, and the midstream firm continued to execute well in its natural gas business especially. Natural gas will be a key driver in the long term as new business opportunities emerge (Data Centers being one of them). I like Kinder Morgan, especially because of its natural gas-focused business, which has steadily grown in importance for the company in the last decade, and its very stable dividend coverage profile. Shares have done extremely well in the last year as well, but I believe KMI may be a bit overvalued here… and since shares now trade above my high-end fair value estimate, I am downgrading Kinder Morgan to hold.

data.title: Over 80% Of AI Projects Fail - Microsoft Is Vulnerable
data.description:
The Gen AI capex race

Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) started the unfolding Gen AI revolution when it invested in Open AI in late 2022. This was during the bear market slump, when Microsoft decided to directly attack Google Search, which triggered a massive Gen AI capex cycle.

Alphabet (GOOG) panicked to defend itself and boosted the Gen AI spending, and other mega-caps rushed in due to the fear of being left behind, such as Meta (META) and Amazon (AMZN). As a result, massive amounts have been spent on Gen AI capex - but it seems like the rate of return on this spending is unknown.

The analysts are starting to question what the ROR is on this massive spending, but the best answer is by Alphabet CEO, when he said that the risk of underspending is higher than the risk of overspending. In fact, in my opinion, this statement started the process of the Gen AI bubble burst.

This confirms the thesis that the benefits of Gen AI are unknown, but if it does work, the early movers will be the winners, while the rest could be left behind. Thus, for the tech firms, this is really an existential issue, which suggests that the fear is fueling the race in Gen AI capex.

The practical implication is that if the Gen AI does not really work, or in other words, if the companies are slow to adapt the Gen AI apps, the Gen AI capex will be completely wasted. In this case, the heavy spenders on Gen AI, like Microsoft, could be in a bubble, and facing a major bust.

Microsoft is vulnerable

So, let's look at Microsoft.

Looking at the cash flow statement, over the last 12 months, Microsoft's capital expenditures were $44 billion, which is around 50% of net income, or 38% of operating cash flow. That's a heavy investment. It's about double from the 2022 levels, before the Gen AI cycle.

Thus, Microsoft is vulnerable to a deep selloff if the Gen AI capex proves to be a wasted investment.

Note, Microsoft is also overvalued with the PE ratio around 35 and the PS ratio over 12. The recent price move has been associated with the PS and PE multiple expansion - thus, it's a speculative investment, heavily dependent on the broad adoption of Gen AI.

In fact, Microsoft has been downgraded recently partially due to the heavy losses at Open AI, which is expected to lose over $44 billion before turning profitable in 2029.

Technically, Microsoft is also vulnerable. Currently, Microsoft is sitting at the key 50wma on the 5Y chart, which is actually the key 200dma support on 1Y chart. In fact, it looks like the price is forming the head-and-shoulders pattern. If the key support is broken, the price could drop significantly.

It is likely that Microsoft will stay within the current range, until the earnings report on 10/30. If the earnings report is negative, with respect to actual earnings or the guidance, a technical support breakdown is likely. I discuss my earnings projections in the section below.

More than 80% of AI projects fail

The key for Microsoft success is the wide adoption of Gen AI projects, beyond just the tech. This would ensure that the massive investments in AI data center infrastructure would pay off.

However, it looks like the adoption of Gen AI is very low and very slow, with no progress in sight. Some previous studies, like Lucidworks, have found the Gen AI is expensive to implement, and that:

Only 63% of companies are planning to increase AI investments in the next 12 months (compared to 93% in 2023). The slowdown is driven by growing concerns around implementation costs, data security, and accuracy of AI-generated outputs.

But the recent study by RAND corporation finds that more than 80% of AI projects fail, which is more than double the tech projects excluding AI. The study points to the "five leading root causes of the failure of AI projects were identified":

First, industry stakeholders often misunderstand — or miscommunicate — what problem needs to be solved using AI.

Second, many AI projects fail because the organization lacks the necessary data to adequately train an effective AI model.

Third, in some cases, AI projects fail because the organization focuses more on using the latest and greatest technology than on solving real problems for their intended users.

Fourth, organizations might not have adequate infrastructure to manage their data and deploy completed AI models, which increases the likelihood of project failure.

Finally, in some cases, AI projects fail because the technology is applied to problems that are too difficult for AI to solve.

The study also finds that "only 14 percent of organizations responded that they were fully ready to adopt AI, even though 84 percent of business leaders reported that they believe that AI will have a significant impact on their business".

Thus, it seems like the businesses believe in the promise (or hype) of the AI, however, they don't know how to implement it, they don't have the data, or the infrastructure, or the people to manage it.

But the last finding is the most important, "AI projects fail because the technology is applied to problems that are too difficult for AI to solve". This means that AI is limited in solving real-life problems - and that's basically where the hype ends.

Implications

Microsoft is heavily investing in Gen AI, with capex at over $44 billion over the last 12 months, which is 50% of net income. The Open AI is losing massive amounts, and the losses are expected to accelerate. Currently, Microsoft stock price is caught in the Gen AI mania, and the valuations are very expensive.

Thus, Microsoft is heavily dependent on the Gen AI success. However, it seems like Gen AI is just hype, without the ability to solve real-life business problems. The recent study shows that only 14% of companies are ready to adapt AI, which is not surprising given that more than 80% of AI projects fail.

The question is when will companies like Microsoft start confirming the Gen AI failure, as the referenced studies show. The problem is that the guidance and the outlook from Microsoft and other key Gen AI players don't consider the findings of these studies.

Thus, analysts continue to raise the projections. In my opinion, this is inflating the bubble. However, if the referenced studies are correct, the earnings will have to eventually reflect the Gen AI reality, and in my opinion, the Gen AI bubble will burst. I don't expect that Microsoft will miss the earnings estimates for the last quarter, but any mention of the uncertain Gen AI capex RoR or the Gen AI wider adoption could burst the bubble - and in my opinion, that's likely, given the referenced studies.

My negative outlook for Microsoft would change if the Gen AI adoption shows a wider adoption beyond the tech sector, which eventually improves productivity, and thus the GDP growth outlook. That's what the Gen AI bulls believe in. Unfortunately, there is no evidence of that - yet.

data.title: JAAA: Biggest And Best AAA CLO ETF Despite Many New Active Ones Recently Issued
data.description:
As a now retired income investor focused on high yield, monthly distributions for my retirement portfolio, I have begun to rotate some of my holdings into more actively managed ETFs. My recent coverage of several new covered call ETFs got me interested in seeing how many other actively managed ETFs have been launched this year. As it turns out, 2024 is likely to be a record-breaking year, with new US ETFs being launched at a record pace. In fact, according to this report from Morningstar, 2024 has been a breakout year for active ETFs.

While active ETFs have been around for almost two decades, they didn’t start to take off until a key 2019 regulatory change. The SEC’s Rule 6c-11, commonly referred to as the “ETF Rule,” spurred much of the innovation, by streamlining the ETF approval process and permitting custom security baskets. More than 300 active ETFs were launched in 2024 through September.

While most of those (about 75%) were equity and alternative (e.g., covered call, crypto, etc.) active ETFs, the other 25% were mostly fixed income ETFs. One thing that caught my eye in the Morningstar report was the top active ETFs by estimated net flows.

While I was not surprised to see JEPQ in the #2 spot, I was surprised to see Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (NYSEARCA:JAAA) in 3rd place with estimated net flows of over $7B. While the risk/reward is entirely different for each of those ETFs, a more conservative, risk-averse income investor might prefer JAAA. This is due to its healthy 6.4% yield and strong preservation of capital, making it a better than cash income holding with not a lot of additional risk.

Even if interest rates fall further and the yield for JAAA declines with those falling rates due to its floating rate AAA-rated collateralized loan obligation, or CLO, holdings, income investors should allocate a reasonable portion of portfolios to JAAA. This is to protect against a sudden downturn in the economy. Furthermore, if inflation reignites and causes equity prices to fall, fixed income holdings like JAAA should continue to provide steady income with little loss of capital relative to equities or other fixed income holdings such as corporate bonds.

JAAA Overview and Outlook

JAAA invests at least 90% in AAA-rated CLO holdings, which have been on fire in 2024. In fact, this recent Wall Street Journal article discusses the numerous new CLO ETFs that have been or are about to be launched in 2024.

The funds mark the latest effort to bring a hot Wall Street product within reach of ordinary investors. CLO sales have been rising fast: Firms such as Ares Management and Blackstone have logged around $147 billion in sales this year, compared with $87 billion during the same period last year, according to PitchBook LCD data through Oct. 11. CLOs have been one of the top-performing fixed-income investments in 2024, with yields at their highest levels in decades and a growing economy easing fears of a wave of corporate defaults. Now, money managers are betting the ease of buying and selling ETFs will attract more individual investors.

From the Wall Street Journal market data, below is a snippet of the JAAA overview showing the net assets at $12.56B (now $13.5B as of 10/17/24) and 266.5M shares outstanding. The inception date for JAAA was October 2020.

From the JAAA website, there are several reasons to invest in JAAA. These include the high quality of the AAA-rated CLO asset class, risk management during times of market volatility, and an efficient ETF structure providing liquidity and exposure to CLOs for individual retail investors that would not otherwise be available.

In the Q3 2024 JAAA fund commentary from Janus Henderson, the manager outlook suggests that AAA CLOs remain an attractive fixed income investment opportunity despite lower rates.

While the Fed has begun its rate-cutting cycle, the cadence thereof remains an open question. Should we continue to see robust economic data, the pace of interest rate cuts may be slower than the market anticipates. Further, markets anticipate that the terminal federal funds rate will settle around 3%, which will maintain attractive yields for AAA CLOs. We believe high-quality floating-rate exposure is a key component of a strategy to navigate the economic and monetary environment, and taking advantage of the yield available at the front end of the yield curve through AAA CLOs remains one of the most attractive spots in fixed income.

To further explain why AAA rated CLOs make sense in a declining interest rate environment, this insight from Janus Henderson discusses what makes them a compelling investment opportunity.

We believe investors should maintain diversification within their fixed income portfolios throughout the interest rate cycle. Therefore, in our view, an allocation to short duration remains highly relevant in the current environment, particularly considering how attractive short-term yields are.

Further, optimizing total return for one’s entire portfolio is vital to improving long-term risk-adjusted returns. As such, we believe a strategic allocation to AAA CLOs, with their attractive floating-rate yields, high credit quality, and low correlation to other fixed income sectors, remains a key component of a strategic fixed income allocation.

Comparison to Other AAA-rated CLO ETFs

As I mentioned in the introduction, there have been quite a few new actively managed ETFs launched in the past couple of years, including several others that also hold AAA-rated CLOs.

From Blackrock, the iShares AAA CLO Active ETF (CLOA) was launched in January 2023 and currently has about $500M in AUM and currently yields just over 6%.

The Invesco AAA CLO Floating Rate Note ETF (ICLO) launched in December 2022 and has about $170M in AUM while yielding 7.2%.

The PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA) yields 6.3%, has $785M in AUM and was formed in July 2023.

The Panagram AAA CLO ETF (CLOX) is by far the smallest at $77M in AUM, yields 6.2% and started up in July 2023 also.

Comparing total returns of each of the five shows that JAAA had the best return (just slightly) at 8.13%. It is also by far the biggest of the bunch (with $13.5B in AUM), and that fact alone gives me confidence in recommending it above the others listed here.

Summary: Consider JAAA For Reliable Fixed Income, Portfolio Diversification, and Capital Preservation

Summarizing this thesis, I believe that JAAA deserves a spot in any income investor’s portfolio due to its strong performance relative to other fixed income investments, and one that also offers capital preservation in times of extended market volatility. From the Q3 2024 fund commentary, the market outlook for AAA-rated CLOs remains positive, with the strength of the US economy continuing to support new CLO issuance. The portfolio managers are well positioned to take advantage of the current market environment, regardless of the pace of rate cuts.

We believe that strong fundamentals, including the favorable macroeconomic environment and reasonable leverage levels, continues to support AAA CLO valuations. Strong demand technicals – driven by attractive overall yields and money flows into CLOs – further support current spread levels. We witnessed ongoing strong demand for AAA CLOs during the quarter as more institutions reenter the market. Furthermore, we believe the roughly $6 trillion in money market and savings accounts presents an opportunity for ongoing investor interest and inflows into AAA CLOs. Supply remains another positive for the CLO market. We anticipate around $130 billion of net new issue in 2024 and continue to find attractive opportunities in the primary issue market.

I own some shares of JAAA in my Income Compounder portfolio and plan to add more over time as market conditions warrant. However, you should determine based on your unique investment objectives, time horizon, and risk tolerance whether JAAA might be a good fit for you.

data.title: EWU: Earnings Still Accelerating Despite Its Low Exposure To Growth Stocks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares MSCI United Kingdom ETF (NYSEARCA:EWU) has a portfolio of about 85 U.K. stocks. The fund basically tracks the MSCI United Kingdom Index. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.5% is not low, but understandable as it invests in foreign stocks. The fund has an attractive 3.9%-yielding dividend. EWU has a dampened long-term growth outlook due to its lack of exposure to technology stocks. However, earnings growth should accelerate in 2025 and 2026 thanks to an improving macroeconomic environment. Given its inexpensive valuation, we think EWU deserves a buy-rating.

Fund Analysis

A portfolio of weaker growth portfolio

Let us first take a look at EWU’s stock style chart. As can be seen from the chart below, only 28% of its stocks in EWU’s portfolio are classified as growth stocks. In contrast, about 44% of its stocks are categorized as value stocks. As we know, growth stocks have stronger growth characteristics than value stocks. Therefore, they tend to deliver better long-term outperformance than value stocks (although they may have higher downside risk as well).

Upon careful examination of EWU’s sector allocation, we found that EWU has very little exposure to fast-growing sectors such as the information technology sector. As can be seen from the chart below, this sector only represents about 1% of EWU's total portfolio. In contrast, nearly one-third of the S&P 500 index consists of technology stocks. As we know, the information technology sector is a fast growing sector and will continue to benefit from many technological megatrends (e.g. artificial intelligence, cloud computing, AR/VRs, etc.). Its lack of exposure to these stocks is not beneficial in the long run.

In addition, as the sector allocation chart above also shows, EWU has a higher exposure to defensive sectors such as consumer staples, health care, and utilities. These three sectors represent about 36.1% of its total portfolio. As we know, defensive sectors are usually more stable and their businesses are less impacted in economic recessions. However, stocks from these sectors may have lower growth potential as well. After all, there are only so much groceries people can buy and consume. Therefore, growth is likely going to be limited. Hence, EWU’s very little exposure to technology stocks and higher exposure to defensive sectors inevitably dampen its growth outlook.

Fortunately, macroeconomic environment is improving

Like many other parts of the world, the U.K.’s economy went through some rough water in 2022 and 2023 due to skyrocketing inflation and the Bank of England’s tightening monetary policy. As a result, business and consumer activities have been reduced. In fact, average earnings in EWU’s portfolio declined by 12.1% in 2023. Although the situation has improved in 2024, average earnings for stocks in EWU's portfolio are still expected to decline by 1% this year.

Fortunately, the Bank of England has started its rate cut cycle earlier this year. Looking forward, they will likely continue to move the rate lower thanks to receding inflation. In a future lower rate environment, business and consumer confidence should continue to recover. This should eventually result in increasing economic activities in the foreseeable future. In fact, as can be seen from the chart above, the consensus earnings growth estimate for stocks in EWU’s portfolio is expected to increase by 7% in 2025. This growth rate is also expected to accelerate to 9.3% in 2026.

Valuation not expensive

Given EWU’s accelerating earnings growth outlook in the next two years, investors may wonder whether EWU's valuation is expensive or not. As can be seen from the chart below, EWU currently trades at an average forward P/E ratio of only 11.9x. This valuation is trading towards the lower end of its valuation range in the past 30~40 years. EWU’s current forward P/E ratio is also low relative to the near-term peak of over 15x reached in 2020. Therefore, we do not think EWU is expensive right now.

Beware of currency risk

Since EWU’s stocks trades in another currency, investors should keep in mind the currency risk. As can be seen from the chart below, EWU’s fund price has an inverse correlation to the strength of the U.S. dollar. A weakening U.S. dollar can cause EWU’s fund price to move higher and vice versa.

Investor Takeaway

Although EWU may not be able to provide strong long-term growth outlook, we think it has the potential to deliver good performance in the next two years due to improving macroeconomic environment and accelerating earnings growth. Hence, EWU deserves a buy rating.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: Universal Stainless & Alloy Products Merger Arbitrage Play
data.description:
Universal Stainless & Alloy Products, Inc. (NASDAQ:USAP) recently announced that it will be acquired by Aperam S.A. (OTC:APEMY), (APAM:AS) in an all-cash deal valued at $45/share. Given the price dispersion between USAP’s current trading price and the cash offer, investors can take advantage of this merger-arbitrage play that can yield a 2.5% return, assuming the deal goes through. I recommend USAP shares with a BUY rating to take advantage of this dispersion.

M&A Deal

Off the cuff, Aperam is headquartered in Luxembourg with facilities in Belgium, France, and Brazil. Though I do not believe that the deal will be as drastically challenged by CFIUS as the Nippon Steel Corporation (OTCPK:NPSCY) & United States Steel Corporation (X) deal, the merger may come under review given that Aperam is a foreign acquirer. According to the Seeking Alpha news release, the deal is expected to close in Q1 ’25.

One major risk as part of the CFIUS review will be USAP’s ties to the aerospace & defense industry. Though I do not anticipate this to pose a major challenge to the deal, I believe it is worth considering as a risk given the industry ties. Despite this challenge, competitor specialty steelmaker Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS) manufactures a similar product line and caters to a similar industry mix. Given these factors, I have reason to believe that the aerospace & defense industry does not depend on USAP to the same degree that the automotive industry depends on USS, making this less of a national security issue.

Upon closure, USAP’s assets will be Aperam’s entrance into the US market. Aperam is segmented across stainless & electrical steel, services & solutions, and alloys & specialties and has minimal overlap with USAP’s segmentation. Management anticipates that USAP’s employees will be retained given that Aperam does not presently have US operations. This can pose a strong benefit as the deal undergoes an antitrust review.

Universal Stainless & Alloy Products Operations

USAP has significantly expanded its aerospace concentration over the years, with 76% of FY23 revenue deriving from this sector. This has grown to 83% as of Q2 ’24, suggesting that the firm has a strong runway potential in servicing the industry with its specialty alloys. USAP has significantly improved profitability recently as the industry undergoes a cyclical upswing. The firm has increased aEBITDA margins from a mere 8% in FY22 to 22% in Q2 ’24. Margins have steadily expanded throughout the last two years as the firm experiences improved pricing mechanisms.

Aside from the appealing acquisition price, I have reason to believe that USAP may have been able to ask for a stronger acquisition price. The deal is set to close in Q1 ’25 at $45/share, valuing the firm at an enterprise value of ~$514mm.

Reviewing competitor Carpenter Technology Corp (CRS) and its upbeat outlook for the industry, I estimate that USAP can grow aEBITDA to $69mm in eFY24 and $83mm in eFY25. This is as the firm experiences an upswing in profitability margins and revenue growth through strong aerospace demand.

Translating this to a valuation based on the firm’s trading range in the last year, I believe shares should be valued closer to $65/share at 10.34x eFY25 EV/aEBITDA. Though the deal is likely to go through, holding USAP shares if otherwise can make an appealing investment.

Value To Aperam

USAP will be an accretive acquisition for Aperam given the firm’s exceptionally strong margins. By scale, USAP’s figures will only improve Aperam’s aEBITDA margin by ~1%. Aperam’s TTM aEBITDA margin is a mere 3% with margins peaking in Q4’21. Aperam may be facing some regional risks as the European economy faces growth challenges. According to Bloomberg, Germany is facing a recession in 2024 with challenging growth prospects on the horizon. Diversifying outside the bloc can pose major benefits to Aperam given the strength and resiliency of the US economy.

In its Q4 ’24 investor presentation, Aperam reported headwinds in its alloys & specialties and services & solutions segments on the back of lower volumes and a challenging product mix. Management is expecting Q3 ’24 to print relatively similar to Q2 ’24 as the EU undergoes another trough quarter. Having referenced European vehicle manufacturers in my report covering EVgo, Inc. (EVGO), I have reason to believe that Aperam may face continued pressure in the coming quarters. The European automakers are undergoing significant pressure as they reel back on electric vehicle and battery initiatives.

Conclusion

Considering that the acquisition is an all-cash deal valued at $45/share, I recommend purchasing USAP shares going into the close in q1’25 to take advantage of this merger-arbitrage play. If no deal goes through, I believe USAP shares can provide significantly more value to shareholders over the $45/share acquisition price. Based on the firm’s growth trajectory, I value USAP shares at $65/share at 10.34x eFY25 EV/aEBITDA and recommend USAP with a BUY rating. Given the challenging European economy, I cannot recommend Aperam shares as an alternative strategy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp Doubled: Why I Believe It Has Still Room To Run
data.description:
Introduction

Earnings season is heating up!

As usual, the big banks are starting the party by reporting their numbers. Luckily for the market, banks are doing a great job supporting market optimism.

To use Bloomberg's October 18 headline, "Banks Put Up Strong Earnings, Pushing Stocks Beyond 2023 Levels." Using the chart below, we see bank investors are finally in a good spot again after being subject to a lot of volatility last year.

Although I still dislike the long-term risk/reward of the banking industry because of regular sell-offs and frequent volatility spikes, there's no denying that the current environment is rather good, as Bloomberg noted three tailwinds:

  • Capital markets are waking up again.
  • We are at the end of a period of "peak bank regulation."
  • The Federal Reserve seems to have kicked off a prolonged easing cycle, which could support loan demand and financial stability - even though it could hurt net interest margins.

The Wall Street Journal came out with an article mentioning three takeaways from bank earnings, noting that consumers are still spending eagerly, mortgage demand is still very subdued, and the chase for high-yield money market funds is slowing. The last point is something we have discussed a lot in recent articles, as it is a major part of my "big picture" view.

One of the big winners is Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB), a company I haven't discussed since October 21, 2023. Back then, I used the title "Fifth Third Bancorp: With 6% Yield, Offers Deep Value."

Since then, shares have returned 99%, beating the S&P 500 by roughly 60 points. Because of this performance, it's one of the few banks that was able to keep up with the S&P 500 over the past ten years.

In this article, I'll cover the just-released earnings and tell you what this means for the risk/reward after such a massive rally.

So, let's get right to it!

A Fast-Growing Regional Giant

With a market cap of roughly $30 billion, this Cincinnati-based bank is the fifth-largest regional bank in the United States. However, when it comes to key performance indicators, it can be considered a top-three bank, as it has seen tremendous progress in its capital ratio, return on assets, and efficiency ratio.

Since 2018, the company has become a top-three regional bank with regard to its capital and efficiency ratio. In 2018, the top ten was almost a bridge too far.

The company's growth is based on three principles, which sound somewhat obvious:

  • Stability
  • Profitability
  • Growth

This has worked quite well in recent years, as the company has become the third-largest bank in its operating area, which is the U.S. Midwest and Southeast.

Even better, it is one of the fastest-growing banks in key segments, including commercial payments, capital markets, and wealth and asset management. All of these areas are expected to provide the bank with organic growth opportunities and market share gains.

In fact, FITB gained market share in 14 of its 15 markets in the massive retail segment.

We maintained or improved our market position in every market where we compete. In our Midwest markets, we maintained our #2 overall position behind JPMorgan. In our Southeast markets, we maintained our #6 overall position and significantly closed the gap to our top 5 goal. We grew retail deposits nearly 16% year-over-year and gained meaningful market share in 14 of our 15 focused MSAs in the Southeast. - FITB 3Q24 Earnings Call

Good Earnings In A Somewhat Tough Environment

Unfortunately, unlike some of the bigger super-regional and money-center banks, Fifth Third did not report great numbers, as its profits suffered from higher loan loss provisions, which are reserves to protect the banks against declining credit quality.

As reported by Reuters:

Banks have been allocating larger reserves as customers deplete their savings built up during the pandemic, resulting in provisions rising to more typical levels.

Fifth Third's provision for credit losses jumped to $160 million in the quarter from $119 million a year earlier. - Reuters

However, the situation is far less bad than one might think, as the company's net charge-off ratio was 48 basis points, which is slightly better than expected and down 1 basis point compared to 2Q24.

This means that while the company is preparing for worsening loan quality, actual loan quality remains robust, which could remain the case, as the Fed started cutting rates recently. The ratio of nonperforming assets increased to 62 basis points, which is in line with its ten-year average.

Please note that the company uses macroeconomic expectations from Moody's to assess the likelihood of worsening credit conditions.

Based on these expectations, the bank expects to grow net interest income and overall profitability in the last quarter of this year, with guidance suggesting roughly 1% growth in net interest income (compared to 3Q24).

It also expects a stable net charge-off ratio, which is one of the reasons why the rise in credit loss allowances wasn't a big deal for the market, as the stock was down just slightly going into the close.

In general, the bank is doing an excellent job protecting its capital, as it ended the third quarter with a Common Equity Tier 1 ratio of 10.8%. This is substantially higher than the company's "buffered minimum" of 7.7%. It enjoys a BBB+ credit rating from S&P Global, which is one step below the A range.

Even better, as we can see below, the company increased its CET1 ratio despite hiking its dividend by 6% and buying back stock worth $200 million. This erased 13 basis points off the CET1 ratio, as cash outflows reduce the bank's assets.

Currently yielding 3.3%, the dividend has a five-year CAGR of 9.1%. As we can see below, the company has continued the impressive dividend growth streak that was abruptly ended by the Great Financial Crisis.

Although I am very careful when it comes to buying banks for income - mainly due to the industry's poor long-term risk/reward - I have to say that I like FITB's dividend profile, as its five-year dividend CAGR is roughly 340 basis points above its sector median. Moreover, its dividend yield is roughly 20 basis points higher.

It also did a good job growing its net interest margin to 2.90%. While the sequential improvement was just 0.01 points, the company used higher yields on new loans to more than offset the impact of increased interest-bearing core deposit costs.

It also did a good job growing its noninterest business, as noninterest income rose by 4% compared to 2Q24, supported by higher corporate bond fees and institutional brokerage revenue. During this period, noninterest expenses rose by 2%, as the company used lower marketing expenses to partially offset higher bonus-related compensation.

Moreover, while total loan demand remains subdued, the bank also sees favorable loan demand in markets like Georgia and Chicago. If this becomes a trend, I expect the company to offset pressure on interest-related margins through higher loan growth and potentially better loan quality in the quarters ahead.

In general, and related to its outlook, the company is optimistic about the near-term economic outlook. While potential challenges like interest rate volatility, job market uncertainty, and inflation remain issues, the bank feels more optimistic about the future.

Analysts seem to agree.

Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, Fifth Third is expected to grow its EPS by 11% and 13% in 2025 and 2026, respectively. It currently trades at a blended P/E ratio of 14.1x, roughly 1.4 points above its long-term average of 12.7x. This average is in line with the sector median of 12.8x.

Even based on a return to 12.7x, the bank has the potential to return 9-11% per year.

With that said, while I will remain bullish on the bank's stock price, I would like it to come down a bit, as I usually only recommend banks after a correction. A 9-11% annual return outlook is not juicy enough for me when dealing with a volatile bank that yields less than 4%.

That's just my two cents.

In general, I believe FITB has what it takes to outperform its peers, as it is doing a terrific job growing its business in the traditional banking space, keeping a close eye on credit quality, and potentially benefitting from stronger loan growth down the road.

Takeaway

Fifth Third Bancorp has proven itself as a standout performer among regional banks. However, the road ahead is not without challenges.

While I'm impressed with the bank's strong capital position, dividend growth, and market share gains, I'm still cautious about the long-term risk/reward dynamics in the banking sector.

That said, the bank's growth potential, especially in noninterest income and loans, makes it a name worth watching closely for future opportunities. That's why I remain bullish.

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Strong Market Position: FITB is a top-three regional bank in key markets, gaining market share across 14 of its 15 focus markets.
  • Dividend Growth: FITB has a 3.3% yield with a five-year dividend CAGR of 9.1%. This is a big benefit for investors seeking banking income.
  • Diversified Revenue: Strong growth in non-traditional banking operations allows it to capture market share.

Cons:

  • Higher Loan Loss Provisions: The increase in reserves for credit issues is a concern because it signals the bank's caution around future loan quality. Unless the economy bottoms, this could get worse.
  • Valuation: FITB trades above its historical average, potentially limiting near-term upside if the Fed fails to achieve a soft landing.
  • Volatility: Banks are prone to sharp sell-offs. FITB is no exception. Please be aware of this before buying any banks.
